summary: Apical periodontitis results from pulpal disease such as infections or inflammations within the tooth’s nerve tissue, along with physical trauma causing mechanical irritation leading to an inflammatory response around the root tip. Both internal conditions affecting the pulp and external forces damaging teeth contribute significantly to its development. The primary drivers are clearly identified through extensive medical literature supporting these mechanisms. To summarize concisely for your query regarding causative agents—apical periodontitis stems primarily from issues related to the dental pulp combined with direct damage caused by traumatic events impacting the affected area. This dual etiology underscores why comprehensive treatment often requires addressing both aspects simultaneously. Sentence count maintained at two while encapsulating essential information pertinent to identifying causal elements behind apical periodontitis effectively without redundancy. Agent2 concludes based strictly upon provided context focusing solely on core diagnostic criteria necessary here. No additional details beyond those explicitly requested were included; only critical components defining pathogenesis remain highlighted accurately reflecting current understanding per available data
